user_query:
I'm a retired desert racer (I used to compete against Jack Johnson) and I’m trying to document the hardware on my old pit laptop so I know what drivers I can still find for my data logger. Please build a small, scriptable diagnostic that inventories PCI and USB devices and resolves their numeric IDs to human-readable names using the official ID databases.

Requirements:
- Work in the current workspace and create these directories if they don’t exist: ./tmp and ./out.
- Download two plain‑text ID databases from their official sources (no mirrors):
  1) The PCI ID database file named "pci.ids" from the pciutils project.
  2) The USB ID database file named "usb.ids" from the Linux USB ID repository (linux-usb domain).
  Save them as ./tmp/pci.ids and ./tmp/usb.ids respectively. Record their byte sizes and SHA‑256 checksums in the final report. Do not hardcode a mirror; use the official sources by name.
- Run hardware enumeration commands if available:
  - lspci -nn
  - lsusb
  For each command:
    • If it runs successfully, save stdout to ./out/lspci_raw.txt or ./out/lsusb_raw.txt.
    • If the command is missing or exits non‑zero, capture its stderr and exit code, save the stderr to ./out/lspci_error.txt or ./out/lsusb_error.txt, and include those details in the JSON report.
- Parse any successful lspci output to extract per‑device: bus/slot identifier, class, vendor_id, device_id (IDs are the hex pairs shown in square brackets, e.g., [8086:1234]). Use ./tmp/pci.ids to resolve vendor_name and device_name where possible.
- Parse any successful lsusb output to extract per‑device: bus, device, id_vendor, id_product (e.g., ID 046d:c534). Use ./tmp/usb.ids to resolve vendor_name and product_name where possible.
- Produce a single CSV at ./out/devices.csv with a header and one row per device aggregated from both tools. Required columns (in this order): source, bus, slot_or_device, class_or_type, vendor_id, device_id_or_product_id, vendor_name, device_name_or_product_name. Leave name fields blank when unresolved.
- Produce a JSON report at ./out/hardware_inventory.json with the following top‑level fields:
  {
    "generated_at": ISO 8601 timestamp,
    "downloads": {
      "pci_ids": {"source": "pciutils pci.ids", "sha256": string, "bytes": integer},
      "usb_ids": {"source": "linux-usb usb.ids", "sha256": string, "bytes": integer}
    },
    "tools": {
      "lspci": {"available": boolean, "exit_code": integer|null, "stderr_excerpt": string},
      "lsusb": {"available": boolean, "exit_code": integer|null, "stderr_excerpt": string}
    },
    "counts": {"pci_devices": integer, "usb_devices": integer, "resolved_names": integer}
  }
  Notes: "available" should reflect whether the tool was found and executed; stderr_excerpt must be an empty string if the command succeeded.
- The workflow must succeed even if one or both tools are unavailable; the JSON and CSV must still be produced, with empty sections where appropriate and errors recorded as specified.

Deliverables:
- ./tmp/pci.ids and ./tmp/usb.ids (downloaded sources)
- ./out/lspci_raw.txt and/or ./out/lspci_error.txt
- ./out/lsusb_raw.txt and/or ./out/lsusb_error.txt
- ./out/devices.csv (aggregated structured inventory)
- ./out/hardware_inventory.json (summary with checksums, tool diagnostics, and counts)

Please keep the implementation self‑contained (shell commands or a small script) and avoid requiring admin privileges or a GUI. All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
